questions.questionText: Cyclophosphamide is an alkylating agent that commonly causes:
questions.optionB: Hemorrhagic cystitis (prevented by mesna) and bone marrow suppression
questions.explanation: Cyclophosphamide: nitrogen mustard alkylating agent → cross-links DNA → cell death. Active metabolite: phosphoramide mustard. Toxic metabolite: acrolein → hemorrhagic cystitis (bladder inflammation). Prevention: MESNA (2-mercaptoethane sulfonate, detoxifies acrolein) + adequate hydration. Other toxicities: bone marrow suppression, alopecia, SIADH, secondary malignancies. Uses: lymphomas, leukemias, breast cancer, autoimmune diseases (SLE nephritis, Wegener's). Reference: KD Tripathi — Cyclophosphamide.

questions.questionText: Doxorubicin (adriamycin) is an anthracycline whose most serious toxicity is:
questions.optionB: Dose-dependent cumulative cardiotoxicity (dilated cardiomyopathy — free radical mediated)
questions.explanation: Doxorubicin: anthracycline antibiotic — intercalates DNA + inhibits topoisomerase II + generates free radicals. Dose-limiting toxicity: cumulative cardiotoxicity (dilated cardiomyopathy, CHF — lifetime dose limit ~450-550 mg/m²). Mechanism: free radical damage to cardiac myocytes (heart has limited antioxidant defenses). Prevention: dexrazoxane (iron chelator, reduces free radical formation). Also: bone marrow suppression, alopecia, red urine. Uses: breast cancer, lymphomas, sarcomas. Reference: KD Tripathi — Doxorubicin.

questions.optionB: Nephrotoxicity (prevented by aggressive IV hydration and amifostine)
questions.explanation: Cisplatin: platinum-based alkylating-like agent → cross-links DNA (intrastrand). Dose-limiting toxicity: NEPHROTOXICITY (acute tubular necrosis). Prevention: aggressive IV saline hydration, amifostine (free radical scavenger). Other toxicities: severe emetogenic (requires 5-HT3 antagonist ondansetron + dexamethasone), ototoxicity (irreversible cochlear damage — high frequency hearing loss), peripheral neuropathy, hypomagnesemia. Uses: testicular cancer (cured with BEP: bleomycin + etoposide + cisplatin), ovarian, lung, bladder. Reference: KD Tripathi — Cisplatin.

questions.questionText: Vincristine is a vinca alkaloid that causes dose-limiting:
questions.optionB: Peripheral neuropathy (neurotoxicity — due to disruption of microtubule function)
questions.explanation: Vincristine: vinca alkaloid → binds tubulin → prevents microtubule assembly (inhibits mitotic spindle formation) → M-phase arrest. Dose-limiting toxicity: NEUROTOXICITY (peripheral neuropathy — sensory > motor, loss of deep tendon reflexes, jaw pain, constipation/paralytic ileus from autonomic neuropathy). Minimal myelosuppression (unique among cancer drugs). Uses: ALL (part of MOPP, CHOP regimens), Wilms tumor, lymphomas. Vinblastine: more myelosuppressive, less neurotoxic. Reference: KD Tripathi — Vincristine.

questions.questionText: Imatinib (Gleevec) is a targeted therapy for CML that acts by:
questions.optionB: Selectively inhibiting BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase (and c-KIT/PDGFR)
questions.explanation: Imatinib: first successful targeted anticancer therapy — small molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor. Targets: BCR-ABL (CML — Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22)), c-KIT/CD117 (GIST), PDGFR (hypereosinophilic syndrome). Blocks ATP binding to kinase domain → inhibits downstream signaling → stops proliferation. Oral, well-tolerated. Side effects: periorbital edema, nausea, muscle cramps, myelosuppression. Revolutionized CML treatment (>85% 5-year survival). Resistance: ABL kinase domain mutations (T315I — use ponatinib). Reference: KD Tripathi — Imatinib.
